title: Harivaṃśa (appendix)
consensus_date: 1st-3rd c. CE (khila/appendix to the Mbh, later than the epic proper; core parva ~1st c. CE, some layers to ~4th c. CE)
basis: Brockington, The Sanskrit Epics (1998); André Couture (Kushan-era, 1st-3rd c. CE); Hopkins (~200 CE); Masson & Ingalls (lang. 1st-3rd c. CE); Hazra (later rasa-lila layer ~4th c. CE)
stratum_note: Late addition: the Harivaṃśa is the khila (supplement/appendix) to the Mahābhārata and is consensually LATER than the epic proper. It was already attached to the Mbh by the 1st c. CE (Aśvaghoṣa quotes verses found only in it). Core (Harivaṃśa-parvan) ~1st c. CE; the text grew into a proto-Purāṇa with later interpolations into the early centuries CE.
note: Current median 415 sits late in/after the consensus window; best estimate centers ~150 CE (1st-3rd c. CE), so current is off by >75y. As a khila it postdates the Mbh core, consistent with the late end of the long Mbh chronology (Brockington 400 BCE-400 CE) rather than the compressed Hiltebeitel short window.

title: Harivaṃśa
consensus_date: 1st-3rd c. CE (khila/appendix, later than the epic proper; some redaction to ~4th c. CE)
basis: E. W. Hopkins (~200 CE); R. C. Hazra (4th c. CE on rāsalīlā); Masson & Ingalls (lang. 1st-3rd c. CE); Brockington, The Sanskrit Epics 1998
stratum_note: Late addition: a khila (supplement/appendix) to the Mahābhārata, composed after the epic's core and attached to it; attested as part of the Mbh by ~1st c. CE (quoted by Aśvaghoṣa). Later than the narrative parvans.
note: Current median 247 sits within the 1st-3rd/4th c. CE late-appendix window; no >75y correction needed. Long-vs-short Mbh debate concerns the epic proper, not the later khila.

title: Mahābhārata: Ādiparvan
consensus_date: core epic with later frame/interpolation layers, within the 400 BCE-400 CE window; framing material 1st-4th c. CE
basis: Brockington, The Sanskrit Epics 1998 (~400 BCE-400 CE, four-stage linguistic stratification); Hiltebeitel, Rethinking the Mahābhārata 2001 (~150 BCE-0 CE); Wikipedia on frame interpolations & Spitzer ms.
stratum_note: Mixed stratum: Book 1 carries the early narrative core but also the latest framing apparatus (nested frames, Ganeśa-scribe interpolation, genealogies). It is not a purely late book like Anuśāsana(13), the latest layer; the Spitzer ms. (lacking Virāṭa & Anuśāsana) helps fix the latest parvans, not the Ādi core.
note: Current median 254 CE is too late for the core narrative book; consensus core spans late centuries BCE around the turn of the era, with only the framing layers reaching 1st-4th c. CE. Long-vs-short Mbh chronology applies.

title: Mahābhārata: Sabhāparvan
consensus_date: c. 300 BCE - 100 CE (narrative core, early stratum)
basis: John Brockington, The Sanskrit Epics (1998) — whole-epic 400 BCE–400 CE; J. A. B. van Buitenen (Sabhāparvan tr.) notes less corruption/addition; Hiltebeitel ~150 BCE–0 CE
stratum_note: Book 2 (the dicing-game sabhā) is pivotal core plot, part of the early narrative stratum; van Buitenen observed it suffered less interpolation than e.g. Ādiparvan. Earlier than the late didactic layer (Śānti 12, Anuśāsana 13).
note: Core narrative; median should skew earlier than current 260. Long (Brockington) vs short (Hiltebeitel) MBh chronology applies; both keep the dicing/sabhā episode in the early core.

title: Mahābhārata: Āraṇyakaparvan
consensus_date: core battle-leading narrative, ~4th-2nd c. BCE core with later episodic/didactic expansion within the 400 BCE-400 CE epic window
basis: Brockington, The Sanskrit Epics 1998 (overall ~400 BCE-400 CE, linguistic stratification); Hiltebeitel, Rethinking the Mahābhārata 2001 (compressed ~150 BCE-0 CE)
stratum_note: Part of the earlier core narrative (the action leading to the war), not a late didactic book like Śānti(12)/Anuśāsana(13). However Book 3 is heavily padded with inserted upākhyāna/tīrtha episodes, so the constituted critical text mixes an early core with later additions.
note: Current median 250 CE is too late for a core narrative parvan; specialist core estimate centres near the turn of the era / late centuries BCE. Note long (Brockington 400 BCE-400 CE) vs short (Hiltebeitel ~150 BCE-0 CE) chronology debate.

title: Mahābhārata: Virāṭaparvan
consensus_date: c. 400 BCE–100 CE (early narrative core; whole-epic redaction to ~400 CE)
basis: J. Brockington, The Sanskrit Epics (1998); A. Hiltebeitel, Rethinking the Mahābhārata (2001)
stratum_note: Book 4, the year-of-disguise narrative preceding the war. A core action/narrative parvan belonging to the EARLY stratum, not the late didactic layer (Śānti 12 / Anuśāsana 13, the latest). Narrative-driven and integral to the main plot, contrasting with the expansive didactic insertions.
note: Current median (274 CE) is at the late edge of the consensus window; as an early narrative-core parvan it is better centered ~150 BCE. Brockington long chronology (400 BCE–400 CE) vs Hiltebeitel short chronology (~150–1 BCE): both treat the narrative core as the earliest layer.

title: Mahābhārata: Udyogaparvan
consensus_date: 4th c. BCE - 1st c. CE (older battle-narrative core, Book 5)
basis: Brockington, The Sanskrit Epics (Brill 1998), 400 BCE-400 CE long chronology with the war/pre-war narrative as the earlier core
stratum_note: Udyoga ('Book of Effort', pre-war negotiations and embassy) belongs to the older battle-narrative core, NOT the latest didactic layer; it is earlier than the didactic parvans 12-13. It does contain a later didactic insertion (Sanatsujātīya), but the bulk is core narrative.
note: Core narrative is earlier than the current median 229 CE suggests; a core-stratum midpoint is roughly -150 to 0, off by well over 75y. Brockington long chronology vs Hiltebeitel short chronology (~150 BCE-0 CE): both place this narrative core early, not in the 3rd c. CE.

title: Mahābhārata: Bhīṣmaparvan
consensus_date: c. 400 BCE–100 CE (early battle-narrative core; whole-epic redaction to ~400 CE)
basis: J. Brockington, The Sanskrit Epics (1998); A. Hiltebeitel, Rethinking the Mahābhārata (2001)
stratum_note: Book 6, the opening war book (first 10 days of the war; embeds the Bhagavadgītā, itself a debated insertion). Part of the EARLY narrative core, distinct from the latest didactic layer (Śānti 12 / Anuśāsana 13). War books are the most stable across Northern/Southern recensions, consistent with early composition.
note: Current median (272 CE) lies at the far late edge; the battle narrative is the oldest stratum, better centered ~150 BCE. Brockington long chronology (400 BCE–400 CE) vs Hiltebeitel short chronology (~150–1 BCE) — both assign the war books to the earliest layer. (Bhagavadgītā within may be slightly later, but the parvan frame is core.)

title: Mahābhārata: Droṇaparvan
consensus_date: c. 400 BCE–100 CE (early battle-narrative core; whole-epic redaction to ~400 CE)
basis: J. Brockington, The Sanskrit Epics (1998); A. Hiltebeitel, Rethinking the Mahābhārata (2001)
stratum_note: Book 7, a core war/battle-narrative parvan (Droṇa as commander, days 11–15 of Kurukṣetra). Belongs to the EARLY narrative core, not the late didactic layer (Śānti 12, Anuśāsana 13). Manuscript recensions cluster tightly around the war books, indicating they are older/more stable than the fluid peripheral and didactic books.
note: Current median (272 CE) sits at the very end of the range and ignores the early-core status of the war books; the battle narrative is the oldest stratum (archaic Vedic forms, anuṣṭubh), better centered ~150 BCE. Brockington's long chronology (400 BCE–400 CE) vs Hiltebeitel's short (~150–1 BCE single composition) both place the war books in the earliest layer.

title: Mahābhārata: Karṇaparvan
consensus_date: c. 200 BCE - 100 CE (narrative core / war books, early stratum)
basis: John Brockington, The Sanskrit Epics (1998) — whole-epic 400 BCE–400 CE; Hiltebeitel, Rethinking the Mahābhārata (2001) — ~150 BCE–0 CE
stratum_note: Book 8 is one of the war/battle parvans (Bhīṣma–Drona–Karṇa–Śalya), narrative core / earliest stratum of the epic — earlier than the late didactic books Śānti (12) and Anuśāsana (13, the latest layer).
note: Battle narrative = earliest core layer; median should skew earlier than current 267. Long (Brockington 400 BCE–400 CE) vs short (Hiltebeitel ~150 BCE–0) chronology debate noted.

title: Mahābhārata: Śalyaparvan
consensus_date: c. 200 BCE - 100 CE (narrative core / war books, early stratum)
basis: John Brockington, The Sanskrit Epics (1998) — whole-epic 400 BCE–400 CE; Hiltebeitel, Rethinking the Mahābhārata (2001) — short chronology ~150 BCE–0 CE
stratum_note: Book 9 is one of the four war/battle parvans (Bhīṣma–Drona–Karṇa–Śalya) belonging to the narrative core (the 'Bhārata' battle narrative), the earliest stratum of the epic — distinctly earlier than the late didactic books Śānti (12) and Anuśāsana (13, widely held the latest layer).
note: Battle narrative = core/earliest layer, so median should skew earlier than current 257; long (Brockington 400 BCE–400 CE) vs short (Hiltebeitel ~150 BCE–0) chronology debate, both place the war books in the early core.

title: Mahābhārata: Sauptikaparvan
consensus_date: c. 400 BCE – 200 CE (early battle-narrative core)
basis: J. Brockington, The Sanskrit Epics (1998): the battle-narrative books form the earlier core of the MBh, predating the didactic insertions; whole-epic span 400 BCE–400 CE
stratum_note: Book 10 (Aśvatthāman's night raid) is part of the central war narrative — the EARLIER core stratum, not a late didactic addition; should be dated earlier than the Śānti/Anuśāsana didactic books.
note: Current median 224 CE places a core battle book in the late didactic range — too late. As early narrative core it centers near 100 BCE–0 CE (Hiltebeitel's compressed core ~150–0 BCE; Brockington's lower bound ~400 BCE). Off by >75y.

title: Mahābhārata: Strīparvan
consensus_date: c. 1st c. BCE – 2nd c. CE (battle-aftermath core/main redaction)
basis: Brockington, The Sanskrit Epics (1998); whole-epic consensus c. 400 BCE–400 CE refined by stratum; Spitzer ms parvan-list (2nd–3rd c. CE) attests the narrative books
stratum_note: Core/main narrative layer, not the latest stratum: Strīparvan is the lament/aftermath directly continuing the battle parvans and is included in the early Spitzer parvan-list, unlike the latest didactic layer (Śānti 12, Anuśāsana 13). Earlier than the didactic books, integral to the main redaction.
note: Current range (-300 to 400, med 227) brackets the consensus; no >75y miss. Long (Brockington 400 BCE–400 CE) vs short (Hiltebeitel ~150 BCE–0 CE) chronology debate applies to the whole text; this book sits in the main narrative redaction either way.

title: Mahābhārata: Śāntiparvan
consensus_date: c. 1st c. BCE – 4th c. CE (latest didactic stratum)
basis: J. Fitzgerald (Chicago MBh transl., Bk 12 Book of Peace, 2004) & J. Brockington, The Sanskrit Epics (1998): the lengthy didactic books are the latest layer; Spitzer MS (c. 100–300 CE) shows the didactic mass still in formation
stratum_note: Śānti 12 (with Anuśāsana 13) is the LATEST didactic stratum of the epic, a massive brahmanical insertion of governance/dharma/mokṣa teaching grafted onto the post-war frame — far later than the battle-narrative core.
note: Current median 218 correctly places this in the late didactic layer; consensus center ~150 CE, current value within tolerance (<75y). Spitzer MS omitting/thin on the didactic books corroborates a late redaction toward the Gupta period.

title: Mahābhārata: Anuśāsanaparvan
consensus_date: 1st-4th c. CE (latest didactic stratum, Book 13)
basis: Brockington, The Sanskrit Epics (Brill 1998); corroborated by the Spitzer MS argument (Anuśāsana absent from the ~200 CE Mbh book-list)
stratum_note: The Anuśāsanaparvan is the LATEST layer of the Mbh and the latest of the didactic books (later than Śānti 12). It is absent from the list of Mbh books in the Spitzer manuscript (~200 CE), and the redaction into 18 books is placed in the 3rd-4th c. CE; it post-dates the entire battle-narrative core.
note: Latest didactic stratum; constituted critical text (Dandekar/BORI 1966) belongs to the post-200 CE redaction. Current median 227 sits within the late-stratum band ~100-400 CE, so within ~75y of a stratum-appropriate midpoint (~250 CE). Hiltebeitel's short chronology (~150 BCE-0 CE) would not extend this late; the long chronology is the relevant frame here.

title: Mahābhārata: Aśvamedhikaparvan
consensus_date: 1st c. BCE - 3rd c. CE (post-war narrative + didactic Anugītā, Book 14)
basis: Brockington, The Sanskrit Epics (Brill 1998); Wikipedia/Ashvamedhika summarizing scholarship on the Anugītā as a later didactic insertion
stratum_note: Post-war narrative (Yudhiṣṭhira's horse sacrifice) with the embedded didactic Anugītā (Krishna-Arjuna dialogue), which scholars regard as a later philosophical addition than the surrounding narrative. The parvan is later than the battle core but earlier than the latest didactic parvans (12 Śānti, 13 Anuśāsana). The constituted BORI critical text (Dandekar 1960) excludes the very-late manuscript accretions (e.g. Hall's proposed 16th-17th c. Anugītā material).
note: Mixed stratum: post-war narrative core plus the later didactic Anugītā. A stratum-appropriate midpoint (~100 CE for narrative core with didactic accretion) is >75y earlier than the current median 231 CE, hence flagged, though confidence is medium given the wide band (-100 to 300). Long-vs-short chronology debate does not sharply separate this parvan.

title: Mahābhārata: Āśramavāsikaparvan
consensus_date: c. 1st c. CE – 3rd c. CE (late-narrative closing book of main redaction)
basis: Brockington, The Sanskrit Epics (1998); Hiltebeitel, Rethinking the Mahābhārata (2001); Spitzer ms parvan-list; whole-epic consensus 400 BCE–400 CE refined by stratum
stratum_note: Post-war closing book (the elders' retirement to the forest and Dhṛtarāṣṭra's death). Part of the main narrative redaction (attested in the early Spitzer parvan-list), relatively late within the narrative core and later than the battle parvans, but earlier than the latest didactic stratum (Śānti/Anuśāsana).
note: Current range (-300 to 400, med 227) brackets the consensus; no >75y miss. Long (Brockington) vs short (Hiltebeitel ~150 BCE–0 CE) chronology debate noted; this closing book belongs to the main redaction in both.

title: Mahābhārata: Mausalaparvan
consensus_date: c. 1st c. CE – 3rd c. CE (late-narrative closing book of main redaction)
basis: Brockington, The Sanskrit Epics (1998); Hiltebeitel, Rethinking the Mahābhārata (2001); Spitzer ms parvan-list incl. closing books; whole-epic consensus 400 BCE–400 CE refined by stratum
stratum_note: Closing narrative book (destruction of the Vṛṣṇis, Krishna's death, onset of Kali-yuga) winding down the dynasty after the war. Part of the main redaction (present in the early Spitzer parvan-list), but relatively late within the narrative core and later than the battle parvans; not the latest (didactic) layer. Critical edition is small (~270 vv) with few interpolations.
note: Current range (-300 to 400, med 227) brackets the consensus; no >75y miss. Kali-yuga/Krishna-death framing leans to the later end of the main redaction; still within Brockington's long chronology. Hiltebeitel's short chronology would push earlier (~1st c. BCE/CE).

title: Mahābhārata: Mahāprasthānikaparvan
consensus_date: 1st c. BCE - 4th c. CE (late narrative redaction within whole-epic span 400 BCE-400 CE)
basis: J. L. Brockington, 'The Sanskrit Epics' (1998); whole-epic consensus ~400 BCE-400 CE; cf. Hiltebeitel, 'Rethinking the Mahābhārata' (2001)
stratum_note: Book 17 (the short 3-chapter 'Great Journey') belongs to the post-war closing narrative cluster (Books 14-18), NOT the earliest battle-narrative core and NOT the latest didactic layer (Śānti 12, Anuśāsana 13, the latest stratum). It is a later narrative-frame ending, datable within the redaction reaching present form by the early Gupta period (~400 CE). No book-specific scholarly date exists for this short parvan, so the whole-epic consensus is used, refined as 'late narrative, post-core.'
note: Current median 189 CE is plausibly within range but the consensus span is much wider and centers earlier; long-chronology Brockington 400 BCE-400 CE vs Hiltebeitel's short ~150 BCE-0 CE debate applies to the epic as a whole. Flagged because current median diverges >75y from the consensus midpoint.

title: Mahābhārata: Svargārohaṇaparvan
consensus_date: c. 1st c. BCE – 4th c. CE (concluding frame book, within the late narrative/redactional stratum)
basis: J. Brockington, The Sanskrit Epics (1998): whole MBh elaborated continuously 400 BCE–400 CE; concluding frame books belong to the later redactional layering
stratum_note: Book 18 is the short concluding 'ascent to heaven' coda of the frame narrative — not the early battle core; it belongs with the later frame/redaction (Yudhiṣṭhira's final test), post-dating the central war books.
note: Current median 215 sits well inside the consensus span; the concluding frame coda is a late element, so a late-centered date is appropriate. Long-vs-short MBh debate (Brockington's broad 400 BCE–400 CE vs Hiltebeitel's compressed ~150 BCE–0 CE) leaves wide bounds.

title: Rāmāyaṇa: 1. Bālakāṇḍa
consensus_date: 1st c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (late addition, Book 1)
basis: J. L. Brockington, 'The Sanskrit Epics' (1998) / 'Righteous Rāma' (1985); also Goldman, Princeton Rāmāyaṇa intro
stratum_note: LATE addition, not part of the original core. Since Jacobi, consensus (Brockington, Goldman) holds Books 2-6 to be the older core (~5th/4th c. BCE); Books 1 (Bālakāṇḍa) and 7 are the latest layer. Brockington assigns both Book 1 and Book 7 to his stage 3, later than the rest of the poem; the Bālakāṇḍa introduces Rāma's divinity/Viṣṇu-avatāra frame absent from the human-hero core.
note: Current median 180 CE sits too late within the stratum; the late-addition layer centers ~1st c. BCE-1st c. CE. Dating the Baroda critical text's Bālakāṇḍa stratum, not an oral ur-epic.

title: Rāmāyaṇa: 2. Ayodhyākāṇḍa
consensus_date: 5th/4th c. BCE onward, present form by early centuries CE (old core, Book 2)
basis: John Brockington, The Sanskrit Epics (1998) — Books 2–6 = stage-2 'original core'; Goldman & Pollock (Princeton Rāmāyaṇa, Vol. 2 Ayodhyākāṇḍa intro): core no later than 5th/6th c. BCE, oldest version ~500 BCE
stratum_note: Ayodhyākāṇḍa is one of the five central books (2–6) forming the old narrative core, earlier than the framing Bāla (1) and Uttara (7) kāṇḍas (later additions). This is the Baroda CRITICAL EDITION constituted text (Vaidya 1962), so dating reflects the core-stratum constituted text directly.
note: Current median (129 CE) too late for a core book; the core narrative was largely complete in the BCE range and reached present constituted form by the early centuries CE. Consensus midpoint ~100 BCE; off by >75y.

title: Rāmāyaṇa: 2. Ayodhyākāṇḍa
consensus_date: 5th/4th c. BCE onward, present form by early centuries CE (old core, Book 2)
basis: John Brockington, The Sanskrit Epics (1998) — Books 2–6 = stage-2 'original core'; Goldman & Pollock (Princeton Rāmāyaṇa, Vol. 2 Ayodhyākāṇḍa intro): core no later than 5th/6th c. BCE, oldest version ~500 BCE
stratum_note: Ayodhyākāṇḍa is one of the five central books (2–6) forming the old narrative core of Vālmīki's Rāmāyaṇa, demonstrably earlier than the framing Bāla (1) and Uttara (7) kāṇḍas which are 1st c. BCE–3rd c. CE additions. This GRETIL item is the SOUTHERN-RECENSION Ayodhyākāṇḍa (Krishnacharya 1905), not the Baroda critical text, but it transmits the same core stratum.
note: Current median (97 CE) is too late for a core book whose composition centers in the BCE range; core narrative was largely fixed by the early centuries CE. Brockington dates the core stratum ~5th/4th c. BCE; consensus midpoint ~100 BCE.

title: Rāmāyaṇa: 3. Araṇyakāṇḍa
consensus_date: 5th/4th c. BCE core onward, constituted by ~turn of era (older core, Book 3)
basis: John Brockington, The Sanskrit Epics (1998); Robert Goldman, Princeton Rāmāyaṇa intro (1984)
stratum_note: Araṇyakāṇḍa belongs to the older core (Books 2-6); Goldman/Brockington place core composition no later than 6th/5th c. BCE, well before the late additions Books 1 (Bāla) and 7 (Uttara). Core stage runs from ~5th/4th c. BCE with the constituted critical text reaching form by the early centuries CE.
note: Current median (22 CE) sits at the very late end of the core range, skewing too late vs. consensus midpoint (~-150). Core stratum, not a late addition; should be pulled earlier toward the 5th/4th c. BCE core.

title: Rāmāyaṇa: 3. Araṇyakāṇḍa
consensus_date: 5th/4th c. BCE core onward, constituted by ~turn of era (older core, Book 3)
basis: John Brockington, The Sanskrit Epics (1998); Robert Goldman, Princeton Rāmāyaṇa intro (1984)
stratum_note: Same kāṇḍa as ram_03_u (Araṇyakāṇḍa), part of the older core Books 2-6. Predates the late Books 1 and 7. Core composition ~5th/4th c. BCE onward per Goldman (omission of Buddhism/Magadha) and Brockington.
note: Current median (85 CE) is well into the early CE, ~235y later than the consensus core midpoint (~-150). Core stratum; correction earlier toward the 5th/4th c. BCE.

title: Rāmāyaṇa: 4. Kiṣkindhākāṇḍa
consensus_date: 5th/4th c. BCE core onward, constituted by ~turn of era (older core, Book 4)
basis: John Brockington, The Sanskrit Epics (1998); Robert Goldman, Princeton Rāmāyaṇa intro (1984)
stratum_note: Kiṣkindhākāṇḍa is part of the older core (Books 2-6), the apparently old layer that excludes the later Books 1 (Bāla) and 7 (Uttara). Goldman/Brockington date the core no later than 6th/5th c. BCE; constituted critical text reaches form by the early centuries CE.
note: Current median (93 CE) is ~240y later than the consensus core midpoint (~-150), placing this core book in the early CE. Core stratum; should shift earlier toward 5th/4th c. BCE.

title: Rāmāyaṇa: 5. Sundarakāṇḍa
consensus_date: 5th/4th c. BCE onward, present form by early centuries CE (old core, Book 5)
basis: John Brockington, The Sanskrit Epics (1998) — Sundarakāṇḍa is among the five central books (2–6) classed as the stage-2 'original core'; Goldman/Sutherland (Princeton Rāmāyaṇa, Vol. 5 Sundarakāṇḍa): core composed by ~500 BCE, not later than 5th/6th c. BCE
stratum_note: Sundarakāṇḍa is one of the five core kāṇḍas (2–6) constituting the oldest layer of the epic, where Rāma is portrayed as a human prince; clearly earlier than the deifying Bāla (1) and Uttara (7) kāṇḍas (later additions, 1st c. BCE–3rd c. CE). Baroda critical edition (Jhala 1966).
note: Current median (111 CE) too late for a core book; Brockington/Goldman place the core stratum chiefly in the BCE range. Consensus midpoint ~100 BCE; off by >75y.

title: Rāmāyaṇa: 7. Uttarakāṇḍa
consensus_date: 1st c. BCE - 3rd c. CE (latest addition, Book 7)
basis: J. L. Brockington, 'The Sanskrit Epics' (1998); R. Goldman & S. Sutherland Goldman, Princeton Rāmāyaṇa Vol. VII Uttarakāṇḍa (2017)
stratum_note: LATEST addition together with Book 1; assigned by Brockington to his stage 3, demonstrably later than the core Books 2-6 (~5th/4th c. BCE) on linguistic, stylistic and narrative grounds (Sītā's banishment, Śambūka episode, Rāma's full divinity). Goldman & Goldman's Princeton introduction treats it as a late, post-core continuation appended to the poem.
note: Current median 190 CE is at the late edge of the consensus; the late-addition stratum centers ~1st c. CE. Dating the Baroda critical-edition Uttarakāṇḍa stratum, not an ur-epic.
